name: Statistics of Grouped Data — Mean, Median, Mode
whatIsIt: When data is bundled into class intervals (like marks 0–10, 10–20…), we summarise it with three averages. MEAN (direct method) = Σ(f × x) ÷ Σf, where x is each class's midpoint (class mark) and f its frequency. MEDIAN is the middle value, found using cumulative frequency. MODE is the value (or class) that occurs most often. An OGIVE is the graph of cumulative frequency.
whyNeeded: Real datasets — exam marks of a whole class, heights of a village, daily rainfall — are too many to list one by one, so they're grouped. Mean, median and mode each tell a different 'typical' story, and choosing the right one matters for fair conclusions in reports, surveys and science.
realLifeUses: Average marks of a class from a grouped mark sheet
The most common shoe size a shop should stock (mode)
The middle income of a town, unaffected by a few very rich people (median)
Rainfall or temperature summaries over a month

vocabulary.meaning: The midpoint of a class interval: (lower limit + upper limit) ÷ 2.
How many data values fall in a class.
A running total of frequencies, used to find the median.
The value or class that appears most often (the tallest bar).

story.title: The Class Marks Puzzle
story.text: The teacher groups the class's test marks: 0–10 had 2 students, 10–20 had 3, 20–30 had 5. 'What's the class average?' she asks Rohan. He takes each class's midpoint (5, 15, 25), multiplies by its frequency, adds: 5×2 + 15×3 + 25×5 = 10 + 45 + 125 = 180. There are 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 students, so the mean is 180 ÷ 10 = 18. 'The average mark is 18,' Rohan announces.
story.extractedProblem: Classes 0–10, 10–20, 20–30 with frequencies 2, 3, 5. Find the mean using class marks 5, 15, 25.

props.label: frequency of each class — the tallest bar's class is the modal class

visual.caption: Grouped data as a bar chart: the tallest bar (20–30) is the modal class; midpoints × frequencies give the mean.

teachingGallery.title: Midpoints, running totals
teachingGallery.note: Mean uses class marks × frequency; median uses the cumulative (running) total.

examples.caption: Class mark = midpoint of the interval; here 5, 15, 25.
Cumulative frequency: 2, then 2+3=5, then 5+5=10 — used for the median.
The mode is the tallest bar — here shoe size 7.

standardMethod.summary: Mean = Σ(f × class mark) ÷ Σf. Build cumulative frequencies to locate the median (the value at position Σf÷2). Mode is the class/value with the highest frequency.
steps: Find each class mark x = (lower + upper) ÷ 2.
Multiply each class mark by its frequency, add them: Σfx.
Divide by the total frequency Σf to get the mean.
For the median, make a running total (cumulative frequency) and find where it passes Σf ÷ 2.
For the mode, pick the class with the largest frequency (tallest bar).

workedExamples.problem: Classes 0–10, 10–20, 20–30 with frequencies 2, 3, 5. Find the mean.
Frequencies 2, 3, 5 in three classes. Write the cumulative frequency of the last class.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using a class limit (like 10 or 20) instead of the class MARK (midpoint) for the mean.
commonMistakes.fix: Use the MIDPOINT: class mark = (lower + upper) ÷ 2. For 10–20 that is 15, not 10 or 20.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reporting Σfx as the mean without dividing by the total frequency.
commonMistakes.fix: Mean = Σfx ÷ Σf. Always divide the total by how many values there are.

easy.q: The class mark (midpoint) of the interval 10–20 is?

medium.q: Σfx = 180 and Σf = 10. What is the mean?
Frequencies 4, 6, 5. The cumulative frequency after the second class is?

challenge.q: For the mean of class 20–30, Sara uses x = 20. What's the fix?

options.label: Use the midpoint 25, not the limit 20

challenge.answer: Use the midpoint 25, not the limit 20
hintLadder: The class mark is the middle.

questions.q: Frequencies 3, 5, 2. Cumulative frequency of the last class (the total)?
The mode of grouped data is…

options.label: The class with the highest frequency (tallest bar)

questions.answer: The class with the highest frequency (tallest bar)

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up a small grouped table (like test marks in 0–10, 10–20, 20–30) and find the mean using class MIDPOINTS × frequencies ÷ total. Explain how a running cumulative total finds the median and how the tallest bar is the mode.

revisionCard.summary: Grouped data: class mark x = (lower + upper) ÷ 2. Mean = Σ(f·x) ÷ Σf. Median: use cumulative (running) frequency to find the middle at Σf ÷ 2. Mode = class with the highest frequency. An ogive graphs cumulative frequency. Use midpoints, and always divide by Σf.

rememberIt.hook: Grouped data: use class MIDPOINTS as the values
rememberIt.unpack: Mean = sum(f x midpoint) / sum(f). Modal class = highest frequency; median class is where the running total passes halfway.

studentQuestions.q: How do I find the mean of grouped data?
studentQuestions.a: Use each class midpoint as its value, multiply by frequency, add up, divide by total frequency.
A range of values grouped together (like 10-20). Its midpoint stands in for all values in it.
